misconceptionSummary: Students find the mean correctly but cannot reverse the process to find a missing value when the mean is given.

lines.text: Averaged the known values — ignored the required mean

steps.narration: The student averaged the three known values, but that ignores the constraint: the mean of all four values must be nine. The missing value must be chosen specifically so the total of all four reaches the required amount.

visuals.ruleText: Find the required total first: mean × count = total all values must reach
visuals.subText: Then: missing value = required total − sum of known values

steps.narration: The required total is the mean times the count: nine times four equals thirty-six. All four values must sum to thirty-six. Find the sum of the three known values and subtract from thirty-six.

Required total is thirty-six. Known sum is seven plus eleven plus eight, which is twenty-six. Missing value is thirty-six minus twenty-six, which is ten. Check: seven plus eleven plus eight plus ten is thirty-six; thirty-six divided by four is nine. Correct.

visuals.label: Required total − known sum = missing value

steps.narration: Ten. Start with the required total — mean times count — then subtract the known values. That is the only way to guarantee the mean comes out right.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: averaging the known values to estimate the missing one. Instead, find the required total (9 × 4 = 36), subtract the known sum (26), and the missing value is 10.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Do not average the values you already know. Find the total all values must reach: mean times count. Then subtract the sum of known values. That gives the exact missing value.
